coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-decorated luminescent porous silicon dressing for theranostics of diabetic wounds

coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yaoxuan
Duan, Wei
Jin, Yao
Wo, Fangjie
Xi, Fengna
Wu, Jianmin
coredata.dc:description:
Diabetic wound healing is highly desirable but remains a great challenge owing to the continuous damage of excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) and degradation of therapeutic peptide drugs by over-expressed matrix metalloproteinase (MMP). Herein, we developed a stimuli-responsive smart dressing for theranostics of diabetic wounds using graphene quantum dots-decorated luminescent porous silicon (GQDs@PSi), which was further loaded with peptide and embedded in chitosan (CS) film. The confinement of GQDs in nanochannels of PSi endowed GQDs@PSi with efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, leading to initial red fluorescence of PSi with complete quench of GQD's blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the decoration of GQDs on PSi surface significantly enhanced the loading capacity for peptide drugs including epidermal growth factor (EGF) and insulin (Ins) which can promote diabetic wounds healing. The peptides coloaded in GQDs@PSi exhibited sustained release behavior and could be protected in presence of MMP owing to size exclusion of PSi's nanochannels. As H2O2-triggered oxidation of PSi lead to weakened FRET effect and degradation of PSi, GQDs@PSi demonstrated H2O2-responsive ratiometric fluorescence change (from red PSi to blue GQDs) and drug release behavior. In combination with CS's degradation in the acidic and oxidation microenvironment, the smart dressing also showed stimuli-responsive drug release toward slightly acid and highly oxidative conditions in diabetic wounds. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated the smart dressing enhanced the proliferation and migration of cells as well as significantly healed diabetic wounds. Real-time indicating of the exacerbation or healing of diabetic wounds was also realized using the rate of fluorescent discoloration of the dressing.
Statement of significance In this work, a dual luminescent nanomaterial was created by hosting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the nanochannel of porous silicon (PSi), which was further applied for theranostics of diabetic wound. The synergistic effect of the host-guest nanohybrid is significant. The GQDs can significantly improve the capacity for peptide drug loading and form a stimuli-response visual ratiometric sensor with luminescent PSi, which can also protect and sustain release of peptide drugs for effective diabetic wounds treatment. After embedded in a chitosan film, the smart dressing displayed H2O2-responsive visual ratiometric fluorescence change and drug release behavior. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated the smart dressing enhanced the proliferation and migration of cells as well as significantly healed diabetic wounds.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the preparation of luminescent PSi particles. (b) SEM image of luminescent PSi particles. (c,d) Top-view (c) and cross-section view (d) SEM images of luminescent PSi particles. (e) The fluorescence image of luminescent PSi particles obtained after activation under UV irradiation (377 nm). (f,g) TEM (f) and HRTEM images (g) of the as-prepared GQDs. The scale bar in g is 5 nm. (h) AFM image of GQDs on mica substrate. Inset was the height profiles along the lines. (i) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of GQD and adsorption spectrum of luminescent PSi particles. Inserts are the digital photos of luminescent PSi particles and GQDs under UV irradiation (377 nm).

(a) Schematic illustration for the preparation the GQDs@PSi dual luminescence system for visual monitoring and peptide drugs delivery. (b-f) HAADF-STEM image (b), the overlay of merge elements (c), and Si, C, O element mapping images (d-f) of GQDs@PSi particles. Each scale bar is 100 nm. (g) The PL emission spectra of GQDs@PSi particles obtained after oxidation for different time in simulated body fluid (PBS medium, pH = 7.4, 37 °C). (h) Typical fluorescence images of GQDs@PSi particles incubated in PBS aqueous with H2O2 concentration of 0, 0.1, 1, 10 mM at different times.

(a) The loading rate of EGF or Ins in PSi particles or GQDs@PSi particles at different incubation time. (b) The release rate of EGF or Ins in BiPEP@PSi particles or BiPEP-GQDs@PSi in PBS (pH = 7.4, 37 °C). (c) The drug concentration of free EGF and Ins (red) or BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles (blue) in MMP-9 solution (5 µg/mL, PBS) at different incubation time. (d,e) The release curve of EGF (d) and Ins (e) by BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles in absence or presence of different concentration of H2O2 in PBS (pH = 7.4, 37 °C). ∗ represents P < 0.05, and ∗∗ represents P < 0.01 when the data were compared with control, n = 3. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic illustration for the structure of smart dressing based on BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS matrix. (b) Photographs of the as-prepared wound dressing with or without the UV excitation (377 nm). (c) Optical microscopic image of the dressing comprising BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and CS matrix. (Scale bar length is 200 µm). (d) Fluorescence photos of the smart dressing on simulated skin with different concentration of H2O2. (e,f) Drug release curve of EGF (e) and insulin (f) form the smart dressing with different concentration of H2O2. (g,h) Drug release curve of EGF (g) and insulin (h) from smart dressing at different pH. ∗ represents P < 0.05, and ∗∗ represents P < 0.01 when the data were compared with control, n = 3.

(a) Photos of NIH3T3 cells’ scratch after incubated in culture medium or with adding CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi, or BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS, respectively. (b) Curve of the cell scratches’ width with incubation time. (c) The viability of NIH3T3 cells relative to control group after incubated with CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles, or BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS film for 24 h and 48 h, respectively. The value of control group is 1.0. ∗ represents P < 0.05, and ∗∗ represents P < 0.01 when the data were compared with control, n = 3.

Effect of different dressings (Control, CS, or BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS) on diabetic wounds healing. (a) Schematic illustration of the in vivo diabetic wound experiments; (b) Representative images and corresponding schematic diagram of the wounds in different groups for 14 days. (c) Statistical analysis of normalized wound area in different groups for 14 days. ∗∗ represents P < 0.01 when the data were compared with control, n = 3.

The fluorescence color change behavior of the smart dressing as the wound healed. (a) Visible color changes of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressings upon ultraviolet at 377 nm in 5 days. The inset scale bars are 5 mm. (b) Correlation of R/B value and healing state in 5 days.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-decorated luminescent porous silicon dressing for theranostics of diabetic wounds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetic wound healing is highly desirable but remains a great challenge owing to the continuous damage of excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) and degradation of therapeutic peptide drugs by over-expressed matrix metalloproteinase (MMP). Herein, we developed a stimuli-responsive smart dressing for theranostics of diabetic wounds using graphene quantum dots-decorated luminescent porous silicon (GQDs@PSi), which was further loaded with peptide and embedded in chitosan (CS) film. The confinement of GQDs in nanochannels of PSi endowed GQDs@PSi with efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, leading to initial red fluorescence of PSi with complete quench of GQD's blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the decoration of GQDs on PSi surface significantly enhanced the loading capacity for peptide drugs including epidermal growth factor (EGF) and insulin (Ins) which can promote diabetic wounds healing. The peptides coloaded in GQDs@PSi exhibited sustained release behavior and could be protected in presence of MMP owing to size exclusion of PSi's nanochannels. As H2O2-triggered oxidation of PSi lead to weakened FRET effect and degradation of PSi, GQDs@PSi demonstrated H2O2-responsive ratiometric fluorescence change (from red PSi to blue GQDs) and drug release behavior. In combination with CS's degradation in the acidic and oxidation microenvironment, the smart dressing also showed stimuli-responsive drug release toward slightly acid and highly oxidative conditions in diabetic wounds. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated the smart dressing enhanced the proliferation and migration of cells as well as significantly healed diabetic wounds. Real-time indicating of the exacerbation or healing of diabetic wounds was also realized using the rate of fluorescent discoloration of the dressing.

In this work, a dual luminescent nanomaterial was created by hosting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the nanochannel of porous silicon (PSi), which was further applied for theranostics of diabetic wound. The synergistic effect of the host-guest nanohybrid is significant. The GQDs can significantly improve the capacity for peptide drug loading and form a stimuli-response visual ratiometric sensor with luminescent PSi, which can also protect and sustain release of peptide drugs for effective diabetic wounds treatment. After embedded in a chitosan film, the smart dressing displayed H2O2-responsive visual ratiometric fluorescence change and drug release behavior. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated the smart dressing enhanced the proliferation and migration of cells as well as significantly healed diabetic wounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is one of the most challenging epidemics facing the world today, which often accompanies by multiple complications and poses a serious threat to human health [1,2]. Diabetic ulcer is a serious complication of diabetes with symptoms of delayed healing, sustained pain, and significant risk of infection [3]. The disorder of insulin (Ins) activity and blood glucose of diabetes cause multiple harmful effects including delayed inflammation, impaired expression of various signaling factors, abnormal neovascularization, and over-expression of matrix metalloproteinase (MMP), which makes diabetic wounds care complex and time-consuming [4,5]. Clinically, the use of some therapeutic drugs to accelerate the healing of diabetic wounds has become an effective treatment strategy. Among them, peptide drugs (e.g. growth factors, antibacterial peptides, Ins) hold many therapeutic advantages over small synthetic drugs and antibiotics in terms of high specificity, good bioactivity, and low side effects [6,7]. However, peptides are prone to be degraded by the over-expressed MMPs at the diabetic wounds [8]. At the same time, effective delivery and stimuli-responsive release of peptide drug are highly desirable for treatment of diabetic wounds. To overcome these challenges, efficient peptide drug delivery system with high loading, stimuli-responsive release, and protection from degradation is urgently needed.

The presence of hyperglycemia and oxidative microenvironment further hinders the recovery of diabetic skin wounds owing to the abnormal production of both intracellular and extracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), mainly H2O2 and •O2 − [9,10]. As known, a normal level of H2O2 (100–250 µM) presents a good ability to promote angiogenesis and resist bacterial infection. However, immune cells in diabetes including neutrophils always produce excess and cumulate H2O2 over a long period which can lead to oxidative stress that can have detrimental effects on wound healing and new tissue formation [11,12]. Thus, H2O2-responsive biomaterials are ideal carriers for the treatment of diabetic wounds. In addition to drug release triggered by H2O2, the stimuli-responsive system can also consume excess H2O2 to reduce injury at the diabetic wounds. In the past decade, several H2O2-responsive drug delivery systems have been developed for diabetic wound treatment, mainly based on ROS-stimulated degradation of polymer scaffolds (e.g. injectable hydrogel, microsphere, nanofiber membrane, and three-dimensional printed implants) [9,13–15]. Although these systems have adjustable composition and structure, good biocompatibility, and high therapeutic efficacy, they are still primitive and provide limited information about the status of the healing process. Since H2O2 levels are an important indicator of wound-healing process, specific and accurate quantification techniques are desirable. Hence, in addition to H2O2-responsive drug release capabilities, ideal smart dressings should integrate biosensors to monitor the H2O2 levels of the wound environment, which provide valuable information for the selection of treatment strategies [16]. In this way, patients can monitor and determine the condition of the wound without changing dressings or visiting medical facilities.

To date, many analysis approaches including electrochemical, optical and microfluidic droplet sensors have been developed to monitor H2O2 in vivo [17–19]. Most smart bandages currently on the market use electrochemical sensors for wound management, which are known for their flexible structure, easy on-chip integration, and convenient telemonitoring [20–22]. Nevertheless, electrochemical sensors usually suffer from easily-contaminated electrodes and time-consuming preparation techniques. Fluorescent sensors have attracted considerable interests by unique merits of high sensitivity, simple operation, fast or real-time monitoring, and visual readout [23,24]. Usually, fluorescent probes for H2O2 monitoring offers instantaneous value under physiological conditions. However, diabetic wound healing is a complex and multifaceted process with a dynamic microenvironment, so it is necessary to design a smart dressing to monitor the ROS level accumulated in wound area instead of instantaneous value during the wound-healing status [25]. Besides, multiple components integrated in advanced medical patches increased the fabrication difficulty and cost. It is somewhat challenging to design a smart dressing via H2O2-responsive fluorescent biomaterial for diabetic wounds management that is capable of performing multiple and parallel functions including real-time monitoring of the wound environment and on-demand drug delivery at the same time.

Luminescent porous silicon (PSi) has a unique combination of several key advantages that promise to effectively solve the above problems. For instance, PSi owns abundant pore structure, good biodegradability, high biocompatibility, low cost, and easy preparation, which makes it a promising candidate for drug delivery [26,27]. Our previous work demonstrated that the pore structure of PSi can be controlled for excluding larger-sized protease that may catalyze the digestion of peptides [28]. Moreover, the inherent fluorescence from PSi is highly correlated with its surface chemistry, especially the oxidation of PSi surface [29,30]. Therefore, oxidation-triggered fluorescence change of PSi could be employed as a sensing strategy to monitor the ROS level in biological area [31,32]. Recently, our group constructed a dual luminescence system incorporating PSi and fluorescent antibiotics, which displayed ratiometric fluorescent responsive property under the stimulation of H2O2 [30]. When the concentration of metabolic H2O2 in the skin wound area elevated, the surface of PSi can be continuously oxidized and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between PSi microparticles and the carried ciprofloxacin (CIP) was blocked, leading to the quench of PSi's red fluorescence and recovery of CIP's blue fluorescence. The H2O2-responsive ratiometric fluorescence transition (from red to blue) can improve the visual acuity and detection accuracy, provide the continuous information, and the surface oxidation can trigger the release of loaded drug in the meantime. However, the majority of the drug candidates are neither fluorescent nor light-absorbent. On the other hand, increasing drug loading rate and enhancing drug stable-release performance are also big challenges. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is also a superior drug carrier benefits from ultrasmall size, high surface, planar configuration, π-π* electrons conjugate structure, good biocompatibility, facile synthesis, and bright fluorescence, which sparks our interest in the GQDs decorated PSi dual luminescent system for the construction of H2O2-responsive theranostics for diabetic wounds management exhibiting both visual monitoring and on-demand drug delivery capability at the same time [33–36].

In this study, we for the first time developed a smart dressing with stimuli-responsive visual fluorescence and release of peptide drug, which enable theranostics of diabetic wounds. The core element in the dressing is graphene quantum dots-decorated luminescent porous silicon (GQDs@PSi), which were prepared by confining GQDs in the channels of PSi. As the proof-of-concept demonstrations, two peptide drugs (BiPEP) including epidermal growth factor (EGF) and insulin (Ins) are co-loaded on GQDs@PSi owing to their well-known synergistic therapy capability but ease of the degradation in the diabetic wounds [37,38]. The as-prepared BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles offered unique H2O2-responsive visual ratiometric fluorescence change (from red PSi to blue GQDs) and drug release behavior because H2O2-triggered oxidation of PSi weakened fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between GQDs and PSi and induced the degradation of PSi. Owing to synergistic effect of high adsorption capacity of GQDs and size exclusion of PSi's nanochannels, the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi also exhibited enhanced peptide loading, sustained release and anti-degradation in presence of MMP. After embedded BiPEP-GQDs@PSi in transparent and biocompatible chitosan (CS) film, the obtained dress could real-time indicate the exacerbation of diabetic wounds through the rate of fluorescent discoloration. In addition, the promoted in vitro cell proliferation and migration as well as significant healed diabetic wounds in in vivo rat experiments were also realized via the smart dressing. We found the trend of fluorescence color change and the treatment effect displayed a certain degree of synergy. Therefore, the visual monitoring and highly efficient feedback treatment of diabetes wound can be realized, which constitutes a promising wound management approach for the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent porous silicon particle
ce:section.ce:para: PSi was etched from anodization of a boron-doped silicon wafer (resistivity: 0.0005–0.0012 Ω/cm) with a [100] crystal orientation in an electrolyte mixture of hydrofluoric acid (HF) aqueous (48% by mass, Alatin Corp.) and ethanol (v: v = 4:1). The electrochemical etching was conducted under a constant current density of 77 mA/cm2 for 600 s. Then the PSi layer was removed from the Si substrate by application of a current density of 22 mA/cm2 for 180 s in an ethanol solution of 3.3% hydrofluoric acid. The obtained PSi film was placed in deionized water and fractured into particles by ultrasonication for 10 min. To obtain luminescent PSi particles, PSi film was immersed in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) for 12 h to activate the luminescence emission, and then washed with deionized water for three times.

GQDs was synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of a natural amino acid, L-glutamic acid. Briefly, 5.0 g of L-glutamic acid was added into a glass bottle and heated to 210 °C with a heating mantle. After the solid L-glutamic acid changed to liquid, the boiling colorless liquid turned to brown in 45 s, which indicated the formation of GQDs. Then, 30 mL water was added into the solution followed by stirring for 30 min to obtain orange GQD solution. GQD were loaded into PSi particles by mixing and mechanical shaking GQDs and PSi for 5 h. GQDs@PSi particles were isolated from solution by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) followed with through rising with deionized water to remove unloaded GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PSi and GQDs@PSi particles
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of PSi particles obtained from Ultra 55 (CorlzeisD, German) at operating voltage of 3 kV. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) photograph of GQDs was taken on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at operating voltage of 200 kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The sample was dispersed in water and then drop-casted on ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid. For atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterization, the aqueous solution of GQDs was spotted onto freshly cleaved mica surface and dried in air. The samples were measured in air by tapping mode on MFP-3D AFM microscope (Asylum research). High-angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images of GQDs@PSi particles were obtained from JEM-1010 (Japan Electronics, Japan) at operating voltage of 100 kV. The absorbance spectra of PSi particles, were measured with SP-756PC UV-VIS spectrometer (Shanghai Spectrum, China). The porosity and thickness of porous films were determined from reflectance spectra using the spectroscopic liquid infiltration method (SLIM). The reflectance spectra were obtained on a USB 2000+ miniaturized fiber spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA). UV-Vis absorbance spectrum of PSi particles was measured with SP-756PC UV-VIS spectrometer (Shanghai Spectrum, China). The PL spectra were acquired by a fiber spectrometer QE pro, ocean optics with a 365 nm UV excitation. The PL images of luminescent particles were acquired by fluorescence microscope (NE950, Nexcope, China) attached with a highly sensitive CCD detector (MC20-C, Mshot, China).

Insulin solids was dissolved in deionized water (1 mg/mL) and the pH of insulin aqueous was adjusted to around 2 by 0.1 M HCl. EGF solids was dissolved in saline (1 mg/mL). Then the insulin solution and EGF solution were mixed in equal volume to obtain a mixed drug solution. EGF and insulin were co-loaded into GQDs@PSi particles by mixing and mechanical shaking GQDs@PSi in the mixed drug solution of EGF (0.5 mg/mL) and insulin (0.5 mg/mL) for 5 h. EGF and insulin loaded BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles were isolated from solution by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 3 min) followed with through rising with deionized water to remove unloaded EGF and insulin molecules.

Quantitative determination of EGF and insulin loading efficiency by GQDs@PSi particles were measured by Human epidermal growth factor ELISA adsorption quantitative detection kit and BCA protein quantification kit, respectively. Briefly, the GQDs@PSi particles were dispersed in 5 mL mixed drug solutions, followed by mechanical shaking of the mixture. Then 200 µL of supernatant was collected at different loading time points. The content of EGF and insulin in the supernatant was measured by the quantification kits and the absolute concentrations at different loading time points were calculated according to the calibration curve of standard solution. The loading rate of EGF and insulin were calculated according to the following formula:
Loading
rate =
M L
/
M T
× 100 %
where ML and MT represent mass of drug mass of drug loaded into GQDs@PSi and the total mass of drug, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of PSi and GQDs@PSi particles
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity experiments were conducted on human immortal keratinocyte (HaCaT) and mouse embryonic fibroblast (NIH3T3) lines. Briefly, HaCaT and NIH3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of about 1 × 104 cells/well and cultured in 37 °C. After 24 h, the medium was removed and 100 µL of DMEM medium (containing 10% of fetal bovine serum) dispersed with PSi or GQDs@PSi was added. The concentrations of PSi or GQDs@PSi were 0.1, 0.5 and 1 mg/mL, respectively. After incubated in 37 °C for 24 or 48 h, the cell viabilities were measured by Infinite M200 Pro microplate reader (Tecan, Swiss) using CellTitre-Glo® reagent (Promega, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing
ce:section.ce:para: BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles were integrated in a layer of chitosan gel supported a sticky polyurethane membrane (PU, 3M, USA). In detail, CS was dissolved in acetic acids with mass concentration of 1% and mixed with (3-glycidyloxypropyl)trimethoxysilane (GPTMS) at a volume ratio of 100:3. BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles were dispersed in the chitosan solution, then the mixture solution (50 µL) was dropped to the adhesive side of PU membrane (0.6 × 0.6 cm2) before the chitosan gel cross-linked. After drying in a vacuum dryer overnight, the uniform BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing was formed.

Quantitative determination of EGF and insulin concentration released from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressings in different mediums were measured by Human epidermal growth factor ELISA adsorption quantitative detection kit and BCA protein quantification kit, respectively. The BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressings were dispersed in 5 mL different types of mediums at 37 °C, then 200 μL of supernatant was collected at different incubation time points. The content of EGF and insulin was measured by the quantification kits and the absolute concentrations released at different time points were calculated according to the calibration curve of standard solution. The releasing rate of EGF and insulin were calculated according to the following formula:
Release
rate =
M R
/
M L
× 100 %
where MR and ML represent mass of drug released into solution and mass of drug loaded into GQDs@PSi, respectively.

The viability of NIH3T3 cells cultured with CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS was investigated by a cell counting assay (CellTitre-Glo) at 24 and 48 h. Briefly, NIH3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of about 1 × 104 cells/well and cultured in 37 °C. After 24 h, the medium was removed and 100 μL of DMEM medium dispersed with CS fragments, GQDs@PSi particles and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS fragments was added, respectively. The concentrations of CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS were 1 mg/mL. After incubated in 37 °C for 24 h or 48 h, the cell viabilities were measured by Infinite M200 Pro microplate reader (Tecan, Swiss) using CellTitre-Glo® reagent.

The effect of CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi was on NIH3T3 migration was evaluated with a cell scratch assay according to previous work. Briefly, NIH3T3 cells were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of around 5 × 105 cells/well and cultured in 37 °C with 5% CO2. After 24 h's incubation, the wells were covered by a layer of adherent NIH3T3 cells. Then three parallel scratches in each cell layer were carefully made with a perpendicular 10 μL pipette tip, following by carefully PBS rinsing for 3 times to remove desorbed cells. Then 200 μL of DMEM medium (fetal bovine serum free) dispersed with CS fragments, GQDs@PSi particles and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS fragments was added, respectively. The concentrations of CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS were 1 mg/mL. After incubated in 37 °C for 0, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h, the plate was plated under a microscope and images of the scratches were recorded by a CCD.
The width of scratches in images were measured by ImageJ software, and three horizontal lines were randomly drawn to calculate the average scratch width.

A diabetic wound model was established to assess the effect of the smart dressing on the diabetic wounds. In this study, 9 diabetic rat (8 weeks, around 300 g) were randomly divided into three groups (control, CS, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing). Before the wounds created, the blood glucose value was tested by a glucometer (Sanno) to ensure the hyperglycemia state of each diabetic rat. After anaesthetized by xylazine hydrochloride injection, the dorsal hair was shaved off and the surface was sterilized with 75% alcohol prior to the creation of wounds. A square full thickness shin wound (about 1 cm diameter) was created on the dorsum of each rat.

Wounds of two experimental groups were covered by two kinds of to-be-tested dressings (CS and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS) with the same size, and the blank control group was left untreated. All of the rats were housed individually in sterile rearing cages, maintained at standard animal house conditions, and had free access to diet and water. The wound areas in all rat were photographed by a cell phone camera (iphone XR, Apple Inc. USA) every 24 h for 5 days. The outline of the wound was marked in ImageJ software and the area of the wounds were calculated by Open CV software. For the group of rat treated by BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressings, the fluorescence images were also recorded by a cell phone camera under excitation of a UV flashlignt (377 nm). Once the fluorescence color of the dressed BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS completely changed from red to blue, the dressing was replaced by a new piece. The RGB analysis and the ratiometric intensity at R/B were performed on Photoshop (Adobe Co.) or color analysis software programmed by Digital Media interactive LLC.

All the data were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance between groups was calculated using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by the Fisher post-hoc test. The significance level was considered when p < 0.05 (*) or p < 0.01(**).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PSi and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, PSi was prepared by electrochemical etching of p++ type silicon wafer in HF solution based on our previous work [30]. The porosity and thickness of fresh etched PSi measured by spectroscopic liquid infiltration method (SLIM) were ~59.6% and 20.9 μm, respectively. To obtain small particles, the as-prepared PSi film was firstly removed from Si wafer and then broken down into microparticles by ultrasonication. After activation in aqueous environment, luminescent PSi particles were eventually obtained. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image indicated the porous structure of luminescent PSi particles (Fig. 1b), which was further revealed in the top-view SEM image with uniformly distributed pore size (~15 nm, Fig. 1c). In addition, SEM image of the cross-section of PSi particles demonstrated the nanochannel structure (Fig. 1d). BET analysis (Fig. S1 in SI) further showed that the specific surface area of the PSi is ~561 m2/g (before activation) and ~47.8 m2/g (after activation), respectively. The averaged pore diameters of PSi particles reduced from 17.3 to 10.5 nm after luminescence activation in PBS solution due to the expansion of the SiO2 layer and dissolution of micropore structure during the oxidative corrosion.

After the luminescence activation, PSi microparticles displayed bright red luminescence under the excitation of UV irradiation at 377 nm (Fig. 1e). Owing to the high drug adsorption and good photoluminescence properties, water-soluble GQDs was synthesized via the one-step pyrolysis of L-glutamic acid [39]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image revealed that the as-prepared GQDs were well-dispersed with relatively uniform size distribution (8–10 nm) (Fig. 1f). Clear lattice structure of GQDs was observed in high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, indicating high crystallinity (Fig. 1g). The lattice spacing was measured to be about 0.24 nm, which can be attributed to the (100) facets of graphite. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image showed a typical topographic height of about 1.4 nm, confirming that most of the GQDs consisted of 1–2 layer of graphene (Fig. 1h). As illustrated in inset of Fig. 1i, PSi and GQDs emitted bright red and blue fluorescence under UV excitation, respectively. In addition, the emission spectrum of GQDs with a maximum wavelength of 465 nm overlapped with the adsorption spectrum of PSi in the range of 400–600 nm (Fig. 1i), suggesting possibility of GQDs as donor for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs@PSi dual luminescence system and its H2O2-responsive property
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the FRET phenomenon between PSi and loaded CIP reported by our previous work [30], a dual luminescence system can also be constructed by PSi and GQDs, a blue fluorescent carbon nanomaterial based on the FRET mechanism. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, nanohybrid of GQDs@PSi with dual luminescence property was constructed by simple loading GQDs in nanochannels of PSi. The high-angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and elemental mapping images of GQDs@PSi particles (Fig. 2b–f) reveal that C and O signals from GQDs was well distributed in Si signals from PSi, indicating uniform structure of GQDs@PSi nanohybrid. When GQDs were loaded into PSi particles, FRET phenomenon can also be observed. As shown in Fig. 2g, initial blue fluorescence of GQDs was quenched by PSi and GQDs@PSi showed red fluorescence of PSi due to the overlap of absorption peak of PSi and emission peak of GQDs. Then, ratiometric fluorescence alternation was observed in PL emission spectra of GQDs@PSi particles obtained for different time in simulated body fluid. The inset photos in Fig. 2g obviously revealed the significant fluorescence color transition from red to blue. This phenomenon was ascribed to the slow surface oxidation of PSi in PBS, during which the FRET effect was blocked by the SiO2 insulation layer. As the red fluorescence of PSi was quenched by the surface oxidation defect, the blue fluorescence of GQDs recovered gradually. Therefore, this oxidation triggered fluorescence color change of GQDs@PSi can be observed. Based on this oxidation sensitive dual fluorescence dynamic of GQDs@PSi, a visual platform for monitoring ROS can be established. H2O2 is a typical kind of ROS in biological systems, which demonstrate strong oxidative capacity when reacts with PSi. When GQDs@PSi was exposed to H2O2, the ratiometric fluorescence color transition process can be facilitated (Fig. 2h). The ratio of red and blue color in fluorescence images was significantly reduced when the concentration of H2O2 increased, which can be distinguished by naked eyes. At the same time, the dynamics of the fluorescence color change increased in presence of the elevated concentration of H2O2. This could be also proven using fluorescence spectra. After incubation for 10 h, fluorescence peak intensity of PSi at 700 nm was weakened and fluorescence peak intensity of GQDs at 465 nm was enhanced gradually with the increasing of H2O2 concentration (Fig. S2 in SI). Thus, the difference of ratiometric fluorescence dynamics can be utilized to the sustained analysis of H2O2 concentration accumulated in biological system. More importantly, once loaded with specific drugs, the ratiometric GQDs@PSi nanohybrid can serve as a universal platform for further theranostics application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peptide drugs loading, release and protection behavior of GQDs@PSi particles
ce:section.ce:para: Along with the fluorescence color change, the slow oxidation also leads to the degradation of PSi, indicating potential as carrier for the fabrication of drug delivery system [26,32]. In addition, the abundant pore structure of PSi and the modification of GQDs on PSi's channels can effectively promote the loading of drugs. Peptide drugs are well-known drugs for their performance in the therapy of diabetic wounds. However, they usually suffer from easy degradation and inactivation owing to the over-expression of MMP in diabetic wound [37]. Herein, EGF and Ins were chosen as model peptide drugs to be co-loaded in GQDs@PSi (BiPEP-GQDs@PSi) because of their synergistic therapy capability for the diabetic wounds (Fig. 2a). The loading and delivery in PSi and GQDs@PSi particles were compared. By combining the porous structure of PSi with drug adsorption capacity of GQDs, GQDs@PSi particles displayed higher drug loading efficiency compared with PSi. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the drug loading efficiency of both EGF and Ins can be furtherly improved the presence of GQDs in PSi. After 8 h's mechanical shocking, the loading rate of EGF and Ins showed 95% and 75% improvement by GQDs@PSi compared with pure PSi, respectively.

Releasing dynamics of EGF and Ins from PSi and GQD@PSi particles were also measured, as shown in Fig. 3b. Compared with undecorated PSi, initial burst release of EGF and Ins was avoided in the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi system. After 8 h's incubation, releasing rate of EGF and Ins from BiPEP@PSi were 51 and 48%, while the releasing rate of EGF and Ins from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi were 38 and 44% under the same conditions. Thus, the confinement of GQDs in PSi could significantly reduce the drug burst release and realize sustained drug release due to its molecule adsorption property (e.g. through π-π interaction).

The enzymatic degradation of EGF and Ins in medium containing MMP was investigated. As revealed in Fig. 3c, the concentration of EGF and Ins decreased rapidly when exposed to MMP-9 (5 μg/mL in PBS). The concentration of EGF and Ins was respectively lowered to 36 and 34% after incubation for 1 h, indicating remarkable degradation in presence of MMP. On the contrary, the concentration of EGF and Ins through sustained release from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles, increased gradually during incubation, indicating EGF and Ins were sustained released to solution. As the porosity of the PSi particles in this work was ~59.6%, peptides with molecular weight of less than ~50 kDa can be effectively loaded into the pores and larger-sized protease (e.g. MMP-9 92 kDa) that may catalyze the digestion of peptides can be excluded by the nanochannels [28]. Thus, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles possessed high drug loading, sustained drug release and anti-degradation performance, promising great potential in maintaining continuous therapeutic activity in the wound area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2-stimulus drug release behavior of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles
ce:section.ce:para: High concentration of H2O2 can induce the surface oxidation and degradation of PSi, which will subsequently trigger the release of drug molecules from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi. This was proven by the difference of drug release kinetics of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi at different H2O2 level. The release curves of EGF (Fig. 3d) and Ins (Fig. 3e) were measured in PBS medium in absence (control) or presence of H2O2 (0.1, 1 or 10 mM, respectively). As shown in Fig. 3d,e, the release of both EGF and Ins were accelerated as the concentration of H2O2 increased. Therefore, the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi composite system displayed application potential in feedback therapy of diabetic wounds which has feature of high H2O2 level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of PSi and GQDs@PSi particles
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the biocompatibility of PSi and GQDs@PSi, cytotoxicity experiment was conducted using two kinds of typical model cells for skin tissue, HaCaT and NIH3T3 cells. As shown in Fig. S4, the cell viabilities exceed 80% in all experimental groups after co-incubating of these cells with PSi and GQDs@PSi particles (0.1, 0.5, 1 mg/mL for 24 or 48 h). When the concentration of sample increased up to 1 mg/mL, the NIH3T3 cells show more than 85% viabilities for PSi and 80% for GQDs@PSi, and HaCaT cells show more than 80% viabilities both for PSi and GQDs@PSi. The cytotoxicity experiment indicated that both PSi and GQDs@PSi had low cell toxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles on cell proliferation
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the biological activity of released peptide drugs, NIH3T3 cells proliferation was tested by measurement of cell viabilities after co-incubation with different particles for different time (24 or 48 h). In order to select the drug-loaded particles with the best therapeutic effect on wounds, Ins loaded GQDs@PSi (Ins-GQDs@PSi), EGF loaded GQDs@PSi (EGF-GQDs@PSi), Ins and EGF co-loaded GQDs@PSi (BiPEP-GQDs@PSi) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4a, NIH3T3 viabilities increased 18%, 53% and 72% after 48 h's co-incubation with Ins-GQDs@PSi, EGF-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles compared to the pure cell culture, respectively. The highest NIH3T3 viability effect was observed in dual drug loaded BiPEP-GQDs@PSi, indicating the synergistic therapy of insulin and EGF. As comparison, NIH3T3 viabilities after 48h's co-incubated with free insulin, EGF, mixed insulin and EGF increased 22%, 56% and 87%, respectively (Fig. S4b), furtherly confirming the synergistic effect of insulin and EGF. However, when the cells culture medium containing MMP-9 (5 μg/mL), the NIH3T3 viability significantly reduced (13% proliferation) if free drugs were directly added into cells culture medium. On the contrary, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi showed 48% proliferation effect in the MMP-9 medium by sustained release of drug molecules to the culture medium (Fig. S4c). The sustained release property of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi can effectively prevent drugs from being degraded by proteases, which is of vital significance in a strong increase of cell number for the diabetic wounds therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the smart dressing and its H2O2-responsive behavior of fluorescent discoloration
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (CS) is a biocompatible polymer which is commonly applied in wound dressings due to its hemostatic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial and water adsorption nature [40,41]. BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing was fabricated by embedding the particles into the biocompatible CS gel cross-linked with 3-Glycidyloxyproyl-trimethoxysilane (Fig. 4 a). The smart dressing displayed red fluorescent color under UV irradiation, which can be further applied for wound monitoring easily by naked eyes (Fig. 4b). The smart dressing has a homogeneous distribution of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles in CS hydrogel which was observed by reflectance microscopy (Fig. 4c). To explore the potential of the obtained smart dressing for ROS monitoring in diabetic wound microenvironments, simulation test was firstly conducted using cotton pads as simulated skin. After BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing was patched on cotton pads, PBS solution containing different concentration of H2O2 (0, 0.1, 1, 10 mM) was dropped on the back side of the cotton pads. Digital photos of the attached pad were taken every 4 h by smartphone camera under UV irradiation (light emitting diode-LED, 377 nm). As shown in Fig. 4b, the change of fluorescence color from red to blue could be directly observed by naked eyes. Obviously, the dynamics of this color change was significantly accelerated by high concentration of H2O2. Thus, the visualized fluorescence color of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS dressing could indicate the concentration of H2O2 in the skin-related area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 and pH stimulus drug releasing dynamics of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing
ce:section.ce:para: The release of drug molecules from the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing to wound surface involved two steps. One was the release of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles from CS gel with the dissolve of CS. The other was the release of drugs from nanochannels of GQDs@PSi with the degradation of PSi. Despite the oxidation triggered drug release from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles, the impact of releasing rate of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles from CS gels by H2O2 was also investigated by measuring the dissolution rate of CS at different concentrations of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S5a, the dissolution process of CS gel in the PBS solution was accelerated by the increasing H2O2 concentration. We speculated that the -C-O-C-bonds between the cross-linked CS molecules were oxidized and broken by H2O2. Thus, the polymer structure of CS was degraded and small structural unit dissolved in water subsequently [42]. Therefore, both CS dissolution and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi degradation are oxidation-stimulus, leading to the oxidation triggered drug release property of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing. As shown in Fig. 4e,f, the releasing dynamics of both EGF and insulin were accelerated as the increase of the H2O2 concentration in the medium. In comparison with that of free particles, the integration of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles into CS gel also lead to smoother drug release kinetics curves, furtherly improving the sustained release effect of drugs. Thus, efficient and feedback therapy of diabetic wounds according to the H2O2 level can be achieved. Compared to the high amount of released peptides in BiPEP-GQDs@PSi system (Fig. 3d,e), the released EGF and Ins only account for 20–45 and 18–50% of its loaded amount after 8 h in BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS, which is a practical format in wound dressing application. The results indicated that there was sufficient amount of peptide retained in the smart dressing after the first 8 h. The BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing would continuously release the peptides and exhibit therapeutic activity in subsequent wound healing process. The long term bioactivity of the smart dressing was further verified by following in vitro and in vivo experiments. It is also interesting to note that the drug release rate could also be real-time monitored through the fluorescent discoloration behavior of smart dressing. As shown in Fig. S6, the R/B value of the dressing after 8 h has a good correlation with the different release rate of both two peptide drugs influenced by H2O2. Based on this point, patients can easily determine the drug utilization rate and effectiveness of smart dressings by monitoring fluorescent discoloration to avoid drug abuse or waste.

Besides the high H2O2 level, many researches have proven the acidic microenvironment of diabetic wounds due to the glycation of glucosides [43,44]. Thus, pH is another marker to indicate the healing status of diabetic wounds. As shown in Fig. S5b, the dissolution of CS gel in PBS solution is strongly dependent on pH of the medium. With high concentration of H+ ions in acidic solution, -OH and -NH2 groups are easily protonated to -H2O+ and -NH3 +, leading to the enhanced hydrophilicity of the gel molecular and the promoted dissolution ratio. Therefore, the weakly acidic medium can also facilitate the release of EGF and insulin from BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing (Fig. 4g,h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of smart dressing on cell migration and proliferation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic effect of wound healing involves the promotion of migration and proliferation of newborn cells (e.g. epithelial cells, fibroblasts) in wound area [45]. Since the healing skin extends from edge to the center area, the migration of newborn cells is significant for wound healing. The effect of each component of the dress on promoting the migration of NIH3T3 cells was evaluated by scratch experiments. As revealed in Fig. 5 a, different cell migration behavior was observed from images of each experimental groups. Fig. 5b was the statistical analysis of NIH3T3 migration assay. As analyzed, both CS gel and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi particles can promote the migration of NIH3T3 but only the scratch in medium containing BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS has completely disappeared after 24 h's cell culture, indicating the therapeutic effect of the smart dressing for migration promotion. After 48 h's cell culture, the average width of cell scratches in pure culture medium (serum-free) and medium containing CS shortened to 75% and 50%, respectively, while the scratch in medium containing BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS were completely disappeared. Thus, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS had best promotion effect of NIH3T3 migration. The effect of different materials on NIH3T3 cells proliferation was also tested by measurement of cell viabilities after co-incubation for 24 and 48 h. As shown in Fig. 5c, NIH3T3 viabilities increased 41%, 58% and 77% after co-incubation of the NIH3T3 cells with pure CS gel, BiPEP-GQDs@PSi and BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS for 48 h, respectively. Compared with the blank culture medium, fibroblasts proliferation can be furtherly promoted by CS integration

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theranostics of diabetic wound by the smart dressing
ce:section.ce:para: The BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS can be considered as ideal theranostics platform for application as smart wound dressing owing to the extraordinary efficient peptide delivery, stimuli-responsive drug release and H2O2-induced ratiometric visual fluorescence. To evaluate the real wound-healing effect of BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing, the wound healing assay in vivo was performed via a rat model (Fig. 6 a). Diabetic rats were randomly divided into three groups including control group without treatment, CS group dressed with polyurethane membrane (PU, 3M, USA) membrane coated with pure CS, and smart dressing group dressed with PU supported BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS film (smart dressing). The wound healing progress after treatment with different dressings was evaluated by photographic imaging for two weeks (Fig. 6b). Quantification of the wound healing ratio of the samples was calculated by measuring the defect area at determined time points, as shown in Fig. 6c. In the control group, the wound had not healed in 5 days. Thereafter, the wound area still remained 60% of initial area after 14 days, indicating that a diabetic wound is a type of chronic wound that is difficult to heal without intervention. Compared with the control group, the wound dressed with CS reduced to 35% after 14 days, indicating that CS showed insufficient effect on enhancement of wound healing quality. As a natural polymer, the therapeutic wound dressing effect of CS have been intensively proven in other studies. In the present work, wound dressed with the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing displayed much quicker shrank with the increasing time and almost healed after 14 days. Therefore, the smart dressing can significantly accelerate diabetic wounds healing owing to the efficient therapeutic peptide drugs delivery based on the GQDs@PSi system.

For diabetes, chronic high glucose causes increased intracellular ROS in cells that cannot downregulate glucose transport. The excess oxygen free radicals are difficult to be metabolized and are easily accumulated in the body, leading to oxidative stress that damages cells and tissues [46]. Bearing in mind the essential role of ROS in the pathogenesis of chronic complications of diabetes, we expected that the smart dressing, which also exhibits H2O2-responsive fluorescence color change property, would have great potential on real-time visual monitoring of wound healing status. The fluorescence images of the BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing were recorded by a smartphone camera under a UV flashlight (377 nm) to monitor wound healing and drug release status. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence color of the smart dressing changed from red to blue as the wound healed gradually. However, the rate of color change differs substantially for the different status because of the varying level of H2O2 in wound area at different time. As revealed in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence color changed completely from red to blue after one day's dressing, indicating high level of H2O2 and severe status at the initial stage. Once the dressing's fluorescence color changed from red to blue completely, the dressing should be replaced by a new piece to ensure sufficient drug supplement. During the next 4 days with a new smart dressing, the fluorescence alternation rate turned slower, indicating the lower level of H2O2 and healing status compared with the initial stage. As drug sustained released from the smart dressing to wound, the wound showed a tendency of gradual healing. During the wound healing process, the trend of fluorescence color change and the treatment effect displayed a certain degree of synergy. These results confirmed a non-invasive approach for real-time visual monitoring of H2O2 level using BiPEP-GQDs@PSi/CS smart dressing, which is highly desirable for diagnostics and healing in diabetic wounds management.

In summary, we have successfully developed a H2O2-responsive smart dressing with peptide drugs for visual monitoring and feedback treatment of diabetic wounds. The smart wound dressing was constructed by immobilization of peptides-loaded GQDs@PSi in a transparent and biocompatible CS film. As GQDs@PSi displayed a ratiometric fluorescence due to oxidation-sensitive FRET effect between PSi and GQDs, the fluorescence change dynamics of the dress (from red of PSi to blue of GQDs) was employed to real-time indicating H2O2 level and the exacerbation of diabetic wounds. Owing to high adsorption capacity of GQDs, size exclusion of PSi's nanochannels and H2O2-triggered degradation of PSi, GQDs@PSi act as good matrix to co-load two model peptide drugs with enhanced drug loading, sustained release, and anti-degradation in presence of MMP. In combination with chitosan's degradation in the acidic and oxidative microenvironment, the smart dress demonstrated stimuli-responsive drug release toward slightly acid and highly oxidative conditions in diabetic wounds. Highly effective treatment via the smart dressing was verified by cell proliferation enhancement, cell migration acceleration, and wound closure promotion. The real-time indicating of H2O2 level in wound microenvironment and the feedback treatment might shorten wound treatment period, save the healthcare cost and reduce patient's pain. The developed smart dress promises great potentials as universal platform for theranostics of chronic diseases because of merits of visual ratiometric fluorescence, highly efficient drug delivery, anti-degradation of peptides, oxidation-sensitive drug release, and good biocompatibility.
